summary: Joel Spolsky posts a brief tribute to novelist Muriel Spark upon her death, sharing a single extended passage from her novel 'The Finishing School.' The excerpt features a creative writing teacher instructing students on scene-setting, advising restraint and patience before introducing emphasis or point-of-view. The post is purely appreciative, offering the quote without commentary, letting Spark's prose speak for itself as a demonstration of her craft.
key_points: The post is a literary tribute rather than a technology essay, showing Spolsky's breadth of interests beyond software
The Spark passage illustrates the principle of restraint in creative work: set the scene before making your point
The excerpt's lesson — don't add emphasis too early — parallels good technical writing and design, where clarity precedes style
money_quotes: You begin by setting your scene. You have to see your scene, either in reality or in imagination.
Don't make any emphasis as yet. It's too soon.
You are setting your scene. You don't want to make a point as yet.
key_insight: Good craft — whether in writing or any creative discipline — requires the discipline to establish context before asserting a point.
